Negative Appraisals.value: I feel like an object, not like a person
I have to be on guard all the time
I will not be able to control my anger and will do something terrible

Threat.value: Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Memory.value: Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.

Maladaptive Strategies.value: ruminate about how event could have been prevented
Situations that put my body under stress

vignette:
This 27-year-old non-binary African adult presents with persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms following exposure to terrorism. Since the event, they have described life as feeling narrowed and unsafe, with a PCL-5 score of 48 indicating clinically significant distress. In day-to-day life, they often feel as though they are moving through the world on alert, unable to relax fully, and increasingly detached from other people. They are single and report that the aftermath of the trauma has made closeness feel difficult to sustain.

Their account of the event remains strikingly vivid. They describe remembering it like a series of photographs, with individual images intruding sharply into awareness, and at other times as though a film is playing over and over in their mind. At the same time, they struggle to put the experience into words, leaving the memory fragmented and difficult to narrate coherently. Alongside this, they hold harsh views of themselves, saying that they feel like an object rather than a person, and that they need to be on guard all the time. They also fear that anger could become unmanageable and lead them to do something terrible.

These appraisals sit closely alongside a persistent sense of current threat. They are superalert and watchful, easily startled, and prone to irritability, angry outbursts, and a readiness to react aggressively. The intrusive memory of the trauma and the vividness of the images seem to keep this sense of danger active, with the present often experienced as if the event might happen again. Their vigilance is exhausting, but it also feels necessary to them.

In response, they spend considerable time ruminating about how the event might have been prevented. They also avoid police and steer clear of situations that place their body under physical stress. These strategies appear to narrow their life further, reinforcing a routine organised around caution and containment. Over time, this has taken a toll on mood, relationships, and confidence, leaving them isolated and preoccupied, with little sense of safety or ease.
